speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- that was Christmas 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had to--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, pieces are l-- like that are coming back to me. Really, I, I was trying so much to forget that now that we're talking about this, it's all coming back to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to-- you went to the Red Cross Station and they fixed up your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wound.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wound. And it was, you know, still, never, never been disappeared. It was permanent-- left, left a mark on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been shortly after that that the Russians arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- they were still on the other side of Bud-- of Pest. They didn't come straight to Pest yet. That-- they were bombing Budapest to give up. And Buda was on the other side of the Danube. And they arrived there first, and then they bombing Pest, day and night. Yes, they did. And they were-- you know, for me, they were strangers. And also we welcomed them, but we were afraid of them because we couldn't speak. But my husband could speak to them because the Czechoslovakians has got a very similar language to Russian. And then he started to exchange like a, a camera for flour to, to make some food. And, all sorts of, you know, the, the-- supplying us with a lot of good things what we needed. But I remember then while we're [? deshelling ?] from Buda, people who, who went out for, for something-- they were huddling to get there and rushing and running back to the shelter. And it was a very, very bad time for us, as well. Also, it was a good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to move, did you say, when the Russians came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because we couldn't stay there. We, we had enough. And we went back to the old place where my sister lived with me before. And from then, we started to, to, sort of, trying to live the normal life. And it was in January. And in February, we were just shoveling the, the snow because we had to do that. Everybody for in front of their own house, there was no sanitary. And my husband came rushing and said, put down the shovel because we're going to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [LAUGHS] we went to the registration office, and of course, it was a, a quick, quick wedding because the people were queuing up for the weddings then. It was such a popular thing to do that everybody who found their relatives or, or beloved ones, they they all wanted to rejoice somehow. And a lot of marriages was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's how my son's age group is a lot because a lot of my friends, and a lot of young girls got married in 1945, and the latest, '46. And next year, they all have children, so we have now a generation of the same age group, practically, which is, you know, good, good for the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after when, when we were married, we went for a celebration at night. It was terrible. I tell you what. We only had in the building-- in the house, I mean-- in the flat where we lived-- only those haricot beans, if you know what it is-- dried beans. And my sister cooked something like a, a cholent, and that's what we had for celebration.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the flat where we lived was shared with two other older women, and it was a two bedroom. And the first night, we spent that time with my sister in the same room. That was our wedding night. I never forget that terrible, terrible times. But we were survived. That's the main thing. Of course, after that came also hard time with the communist and emigration-- not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was staying in Budapest for until 1950. Then, because my husband was still a Czechoslovakian, he obtained a pass going back, like, home, and take me with him. But it was after the communist came in, and they started-- the communist when they came in they started to take away the businesses from people. And my husband has already in partnership a little business because he was a jeweler. And he started to work, but because it was such a dangerous time that everything will be taken away, and we already had a little son-- that was already in 1947. So we started to think of emigrating before they take everything, but we still left something. And then in 1950, that friend of mine who was with me in the working camp, she has got that Swedish passport in time, and she got out of the working camp. And she came also back to live in the house where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't tell us, but they came in 1950 to Australia. And when, when they arrived, they wrote us a letter that we are sitting under a palm tree, and we are already playing tennis. So everything what was enticing and beautiful, and the climate is fantastic, and we want you to come out here. And we are going to send you a-- not passport actually, what was it what they sending this time? What is it called? They get-- they, they take responsibility that we-- A sponsorship--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sponsoring, yeah. Right. And then, what happened is we decided to go. And in 1950, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we went to Czechoslovakia thinking that from then on, we will find a way to get out because there was a, a-- I have to look for the English words for a lot of things. There, there was like a-- like a, a group of people who helping out-- HIAS. It's called HIAS. And by the time we got there, the HIAS couldn't take us any more people. So we had to look for somebody who was taking us through the borders to Austria. And we find a fellow who will take us at night. My son was three years old, and we had to go to-- from, from-- [? leave ?] where my husband taking us. He was called up-- excuse me, I have to go back to this. He was called up to be a soldier because if he consider himself a Czechoslovakian, he said compulsory to go to the army. And he-- of course, he didn't want to go the army, so we, we went to Bratislava, which is the capital city of Slovakia, and we tried to find a way out. And he found a man who could take us out-- of course for money-- and we had to wait until a group of people got together. And at night, we started on foot from Bratislava to Vienna. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course it was very hard to keep a child quiet. And they were a group of people, and the leader always said that if I disappear and you don't see me, don't look for me because if somebody-- you know there were barbed wires all over the border-- and he said, if, if I disappear, that means that I saving my own life and I have to abandon you. So you go on your own. So a few times he just disappeared, but he always came back when he saw that the, the air is clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he cut a, a big hole in the barbed wire. In the meantime, they were patrolling. You know, we heard shots, shots all over the place. And then when, you know, we carried a rucksack-- you know what is a rucksack? Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I carried a rucksack, and that was in [NON-ENGLISH]. And we had about three loads of clothing on each of us, so whatever we could save, we saved. And of course it wasn't easy to go. It was-- it was summer there, yes, June-- no no no, we arrived in June to Australia. It was September, September, yes. And it was coolish already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did is we, we took out a sheet of, made two, two knots on each side of the sheet, and we put my son in. And we carried him because first we carried him and the rucksack. You know, my husband carried, then I carried him on, on, you know, holding him. But after a while, I couldn't manage it. I, I thought, I'm going to die with all this clothing in the rucksack. So we, we made the sheet, and we put him in, and we gave him something to, to sleep. And that's how we got through the border. It was a village called Kittsee. And in this village, there was a, a-- what was it the horses in-- the stable. And we all fall down like sacks, sacks of potato. How long had you been walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: All night long. All night. It was about like the time it was dark, real dark, about 9:00 at night, and we arrived about 6:00 in the morning. All this time and we had to go through the wire and pushing each others through. It was an ordeal as well. But then, we were put in a big truck. And in the truck, they covered us with the, the-- I don't know what was the cover-- but waterproof something. And they take us into Vienna in a Lager also, and we live there for seven months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we couldn't afford to live outside because lot of people bought-- n not bought, just rented flats. And they leave because it was not a very good condition in this camps. What was it? It was a school called Augsberger, the school. And the other one was Rothschild, which was also another school in Vienna where they gathered the, the escaping Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a school-- of course it was a huge, huge room. And everybody got two beds, which was like the soldiers get that open and closing bed. And we got all around, for two or three people, we got all around that blankets. And that was our quarter-- nothing else. And of course we had to go with-- to queuing up with for food and everything. I put my child into a kindergarten. He was quite good at German in a very short time because he was only three years old. And I got a stomach ulcer resulted from all this trouble what been through. Looks like I didn't take it so easy as I thought because I got very sick. I lost nine kilo. I was taken to the, the local hospital in Vienna, and a miraculous way that they gave me an injection to my back. I don't know what was it. Maybe they call it today epidural or whatever it is that they didn't tell me. It was a public hospital, and I was there for two or three weeks. And they let me out. But that stomach ulcer has never left me properly because until today, it still keep coming back. So I am always on medication.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if I can talk about our beginning here-- can I? Yes. Well, we came out in 1951, June. We were taken to Bonegilla, it's called. It was-- I don't know-- it was Victoria? I think it was in Victoria.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was also like an army camp. We got the same two bed, except we had a door, which was dividing us. And then there was very, very cold winter here in June. We had to go out for a shower. And the shower, it was like in a row of, of taps. And the top of the, the building was open. I don't know for what reason. And we were shivering, and, and that's how we did the washing. And then we had to queue up for the food. And my husband had said, that this enough. I'm going up-- we were not allowed to get out of there. But he was trying to get to Sydney to this friend who was already here. And he obtained a letter from them that we are responsible for employment for, for the husband, and that's how we could get out of there. We were there not longer than two or three weeks. And we went to, to see this friend, and we were staying with them until we get into the-- a boarding house where we rented a room. And we started to work. But unfortunately, the luck wasn't with us because my son has had a rheumatic fever when he was already-- we were here for a couple of years-- no, first year. No, the first person who got sick-- I was a sick with thyroid problem. My eyes was bulging. My heart was beating 120 at a minute, and my neck was swollen, and I still had to work. I went to a factory which is called Harrison. My husband was working there also. He was doing the tools on the machine. I was packing the tools. And we had a key money flat in [INAUDIBLE] got this opposite the park. It was a bachelor. So it was one bedroom, and so-called lunchroom together divided with the kitchen. The kitchen was just a strip. And he was working in different factor-- factories until he reached-- went to [INAUDIBLE]. And he did night shift as well as day shift many a times, so we hardly met because I was working in a dress factory, finishing. After-- I never had a needle in my hand, but after two or three jobs, they throw me out. They finally I find-- [LAUGHS] I learned how to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened is, one day that he was going with six other fellows in a truck-- motor truck. It was a closed-- a station wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: They always went and together they came home, and they went to work. And he was sort of leaning against a door-- the back door. They had a collision with a taxi. And he fall out of-- the door opened, and he fall out of the car. And he has broken his vertebrae. And he was taken to the hospital. They didn't even bother to do x-ray him. They sent him home the same night. We were living on the second floor. Me and my friend dragged him up the second floor and put him to bed, and all night long, he was in an agonizing pain. Next day, we took him back. Then they made an x-ray, and they saw that his lower vertebrae was broken. Then I got the thyroid problem-- I mean the thyroid and, and him-- sorry, it was nearly the same time. And my son got rheumatic fever. And he had to be taken to hospital, and after that be home about six weeks in bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the beginning of our life. But he was, of course, not good in English-- neither of us. And with the bachelor flat, we couldn't take him up the second floor because he couldn't walk two floors up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to get somehow the key money back, so we got doctor's order and all sorts of, you know, complication until we got the money back. And we went to a ground floor flat. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And my son was in a kindergarten. But he was so happy in the kindergarten that he kept vomiting. And we didn't know why, why does he do that? And I was very upset and I took him all the time to the doctor. I thought that he must have something wrong with the appendix or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it turned up to be that he was so nervous there that he was kept vomiting all the time, and, and he came-- [CRYING] sorry. He came home from-- I brought him back every weekend, and he started to cry that he doesn't want to go back. And as I cried with him [SOBBING] because I had to take him back-- I had to work. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the beginning, and my husband had to get some compensation for that accident because he is hit on the way back to work. And the solicitor advised him not to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not to pursue the, the matter because he doesn't speak English. And he is still-- there was a lot of resentment for foreigners accepted before the, the actual-- you know, the-- before they finalize.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he agreed in a very tiny amount. Then he started on crutches to go to auction sales, buying for the money little things, and then, that's how we started to open the shop later on in 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was that the compensation money that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what we started with, yes. And we didn't do too bad. We both worked. I could take my son out and send him to a proper school. He was in [INAUDIBLE]. He did very well. Luckily, he became a dentist, and that's how it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's, that's the story of our life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And going through a lot. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anything else you want to know? To ask? Is there anything else you want to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not really. I think I said all that I had to say. I owe a lot to my sister and a lot to my boss because they were very good to me. And I just hope that it won't happen again. That's all I have to add.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was so worried, and that's why I wanted to leave Hungary so desperately because of my love of my son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I thought that he-- I have to offer him a better life. And I hope to go to a country where it won't happen again. Who knows? Magda, your husband died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband died in 1977, yes. He was 58 years old, and he had cancer. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When he should have the better life, he wanted to say that I retire at 60 and I give it to my nephew, my sister's son, the business, and then I want to live a normal life. But he didn't get to it. And since then, I am by myself. Which isn't-- I don't feel sorry for myself. Not that's why I'm crying. Don't think for a minute. I'm not the type. Yeah. I never-- I never ever thought over my life as I do now, and really I'm grateful that you came along and had the chance to reconstruct [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because, you know, you're trying to forget a lot of things. Hard. I just hope that when my, my family living see that tape, will realize that that's all happened for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is there anything else? Thank you very much for coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Magda. Thank you, Magda Hedges.
